chosen_title: US CDC raises Ebola outbreak response to highest level
label: THE STORY
headline: Ebola's Biggest Outbreak Yet?
image_prompt: A lone health worker in full blue hazmat PPE stands at the entrance of a makeshift medical tent pitched in a red-earth jungle clearing in central Africa, their gloved hand resting on a sealed metal supply case marked with a biohazard symbol. The jungle treeline behind is dark and impenetrable under a heavy, overcast sky that presses the humidity down. A bright accent glow pulses from the tent opening directly behind the figure, throwing a long shadow across cracked laterite soil and catching the sheen on the worker's visor. The lower third of the frame is calm, occupied only by dark red earth and deep shadow.
tweets: The U.S. CDC just raised its Ebola response to the highest possible level. Africa's CDC is now warning this could become the largest outbreak in history. 🧵

The CDC runs a tiered Emergency Operations system. Level 1 — the top — means 24/7 ops, deployed field teams, and full federal coordination. It hasn't been triggered for Ebola since the 2014 West Africa outbreak that ultimately killed over 11,000 people.

This outbreak is centered in the Democratic Republic of Congo — a country that has battled Ebola more than a dozen times. But Africa's CDC is now warning this one could surpass every prior outbreak on record. That's a statement from people who've seen it all.

Ebola spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids and has hit 90% fatality rates in severe past outbreaks. Vaccines exist — but eastern DRC is one of the most conflict-ridden regions on Earth. Getting doses to people is a different problem entirely.

What's stacked against containment:
• Active armed conflict blocks health teams from reaching cases
• Cross-border movement is carrying the virus beyond DRC
• Deep community distrust of outside medical responders
• No signs the outbreak trajectory is slowing

The 2014–16 West Africa outbreak — previously the largest on record — took 2.5 years and roughly $3.6B to stop, backed by full international mobilization. U.S. global health funding has since been cut significantly. The firewall that stopped 2014 is weaker now.

A Level 1 response means the window to act is now. The real question is whether the world has the infrastructure left to use it.

Source: Reuters
